planting.note: Direct sow in garden after last frost; in zone 9 sow February–March for spring and August for fall. Vietnamese perilla self-seeds prolifically once established and may naturalize in warm-climate gardens. Barely cover seed with soil or press onto the surface, as seeds require light for germination. In zone 9 it functions as a warm-season annual that reseeds itself year after year.

directions: Direct sow or transplant into moderately fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; Vietnamese perilla (rau tia to) is the large-leafed, flat-surfaced green strain of perilla used extensively in Vietnamese and Southeast Asian cooking, distinctly different in appearance and flavor from the ruffled Japanese shiso cultivars. The broad, flat, dark green leaves with a slightly purple reverse are used as a table herb in pho, bún bò Huế, spring rolls, and salad plates — leaves are torn and added raw at the table rather than cooked. The flavor is more robust and less refined than Japanese shiso, with strong anise, mint, and basil notes that pair well with the bold flavors of Vietnamese broths. In zone 9 the plant performs best in the cool seasons of spring and fall; it tends to bolt and become bitter in peak summer. It tolerates partial shade and is ideal for the edge of a vegetable garden or under taller plants where it can capture filtered light.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Asian Herbs for Florida
Rutgers University — Herbs for Asian Cuisine: Perilla Production
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Specialty Herbs for Southeast Asian Cooking

planting.note: Plant slips after last frost when soil is warm; in zone 9 plant March–June. Camouflage produces attractive vines with lobed leaves and is vigorous enough to suppress weeds when given room to ramble. Harvest carefully with a digging fork to avoid puncturing the skin; purple-white streaked flesh appearance is best when roots are not damaged.

directions: Plant slips 4–6 inches deep in loose, well-drained soil amended with sand or compost in full sun; Camouflage is an ornamental-edible sweet potato selected for striking purple-and-cream streaked or mottled flesh that creates a dramatic presentation when sliced — the irregular pattern of cream and purple pigmentation resembles military camouflage when cut crosswise. The flavor is mild and moderately sweet, suitable for roasting, baking, and mashing where the color pattern is partially preserved; water-boiling dulls the visual effect. The exterior skin has a similar mottled purple-green or purple-tan coloring that makes the roots attractive at harvest and at market. Flavor quality is secondary to the ornamental value of the flesh pattern — for pure eating quality, standard colored varieties outperform Camouflage, but for presentations and table drama it is unmatched among commonly grown sweet potatoes. Cure harvested roots at 85°F and high humidity for 7–10 days to develop sweetness.
sources: North Carolina State University Extension — Specialty Sweet Potato Varieties
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Sweet Potato Variety Trials
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Sweet Potato Production

planting.note: Direct sow or plant divisions in early spring as soon as the soil can be worked; in zone 9 plant September–February for a cool-season crop that persists through spring. Giant Chives tolerate light frosts and can be divided every 2–3 years in early spring before growth resumes. In zone 9 the plant goes semi-dormant in midsummer heat but revives in fall.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; Giant Chives ('Forescate') is a selected cultivar of common chives that produces hollow stems 18–24 inches tall — significantly taller and more robust than standard chive types — with a more pronounced, buttery onion flavor particularly valued for garnishing soups and baked potatoes where the extra visual impact of longer stems improves presentation. The larger stems are also more practical for fine chopping in large quantities. The lavender-pink globe flowers are identical to standard chives but borne on longer stems, making them excellent cut flowers and attractive to pollinators. 'Forescate' is the French market cultivar selected for consistent large size and rapid regrowth after cutting. Like all chives, it dies to the ground in severe winters but re-emerges reliably in spring; in zone 9 it remains partially green through mild winters. Divide clumps every 2–3 years to maintain vigor.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Specialty Alliums
Nichols Garden Nursery — Forescate Giant Chives Selection Notes
Cornell University Herb Garden — Allium schoenoprasum Cultivars

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is thoroughly warm; in zone 9 plant March–May for spring and July–August for fall. Lima beans require warm, settled weather to germinate and thrive — cold or wet soil at planting causes rot. Calico is a pole type requiring support; provide a trellis or bean poles at least 6 feet tall. In zone 9 the vines may overwinter as short-lived perennials in mild winters.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Calico lima bean is a large, pole-type heirloom producing spectacular seeds splashed with deep red-purple and cream in an irregular speckled pattern — each seed is uniquely marked, no two alike, and the visual drama makes them among the most beautiful beans in the garden and at harvest. The large seeds are best used as fresh-shell or dry beans; the meaty, starchy texture and rich, buttery lima flavor are excellent. Fresh-shell calico limas cook faster than dried and have a creamier texture. Despite the striking appearance of the dried seeds, the calico pattern largely disappears when cooked as the pigments are water-soluble. The vigorous pole vines need substantial support and are highly productive through the long Gulf Coast growing season. Heat tolerance is excellent, and the plants continue to set pods when green beans have long since quit in summer heat.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Calico Lima Bean Heritage Variety
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Lima Bean Production
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Heirloom Lima Bean Types

planting.note: Start indoors 3–4 weeks before last frost; transplant into warm soil in zone 9 February–March. Korean melons are fast-maturing and can be succession-planted for multiple crops in zone 9 — spring, summer, and fall plantings are possible. The small fruit size and bush habit make them practical for raised beds and containers. Harvest when the skin turns fully golden-yellow and the stem begins to slip.

directions: Transplant into fertile, well-drained soil in full sun with consistent moisture; Korean melon (chamoe, chamgwoe) is a small, oval to round melon of East Asian origin that ripens from green-striped to a brilliant golden-yellow with white stripes running longitudinally along the skin — visually distinct from any Western melon type. The small (4–8 ounce) fruits have crisp, white flesh with a delicate floral fragrance and exceptionally sweet flavor; the thin skin is edible and the seeds and surrounding gel are also consumed in Korean tradition. Unlike large Western muskmelons, Korean melons are picked at peak ripeness and eaten immediately — they do not store well, and the flavor deteriorates quickly after harvest. Grow on trellises for cleaner fruits or allow to sprawl on mulched ground. The fast maturity (60–75 days from transplant) makes Korean melon one of the quickest-maturing melons available, allowing multiple crops per season in zone 9.
sources: Rural Development Administration (Korea) — Cucumis melo var. makuwa
University of California Cooperative Extension — Asian Melon Production
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Specialty Melon Varieties for Florida
